title: Salt, Water, and a Floating Egg
summary: Discover why an egg sinks in fresh water but floats in salt water by adding salt one spoonful at a time until the egg lifts off the bottom.
learning_goals: Connect density to whether an object sinks or floats.
Predict how dissolving salt changes the density of water.
Design a simple iterative test (add, observe, repeat).

materials.name: Tall clear glass or jar
materials.notes: Tall enough that the egg is fully submerged.

materials.name: Fresh raw egg in its shell
Tap water
materials.quantity: enough to fill the glass about 3/4

safety: Use a raw egg only in its shell. If it cracks, stop and clean up.
Do not drink the salt water.
Work over a tray to catch spills.

steps.instruction: Fill the glass about three-quarters full with tap water. Place the empty spoon and the salt nearby.

checkpoint.expected_state: A clear glass roughly three-quarters full of clear water, with a container of salt and a spoon visible nearby. The egg has not yet been placed in the water.
success_criteria: A clear glass is visible and contains water to roughly 3/4 of its height.
A container of salt is visible near the glass.
No egg is visible inside the glass yet.
failure_hints: If the glass is too full, pour a little out so the water doesn't overflow when you add the egg.
Slide the salt container into the frame so the assistant can see it.

simulation.observation: A clear glass sits on the bench, three-quarters full of plain tap water. A small mound of salt and a spoon wait beside it.
tutor_prompts: Before we add the egg, what do you think will happen when you drop it in?
What property of the water and the egg will determine whether it sinks or floats?

steps.instruction: Gently lower the egg into the water with your fingers or a spoon. Watch what happens.

checkpoint.expected_state: The egg has been placed in the water and is resting on the bottom of the glass, fully submerged.
success_criteria: The egg is visible inside the glass.
The egg is at or very near the bottom of the glass, not floating at the top.
The water level rose slightly and did not overflow.
failure_hints: If the egg is floating already, your water might already be salty — start over with fresh tap water.
If the egg is not visible, tilt the camera slightly so we can see the bottom of the glass.

simulation.observation: The egg sinks slowly and settles on the bottom of the glass. It does not float.
tutor_prompts: The egg sank. What does that tell you about the density of the egg compared to the water?
How could we change the water — without removing the egg — so that it might float instead?

steps.instruction: Add one tablespoon of salt and stir gently until it dissolves. The egg should stay in the glass.

checkpoint.expected_state: The egg is still in the glass and salt has been added. The water may look slightly cloudy or have a small amount of undissolved salt at the bottom.
success_criteria: The egg is still inside the glass.
There is visible evidence that salt has been added or stirring is happening (cloudy water, salt grains, or a spoon in the glass).
Nothing has spilled out of the glass.
failure_hints: Stir more slowly so you don't crack the egg with the spoon.
If the water is overflowing, scoop a little out before adding more salt.

simulation.observation: The salt swirls into the water as you stir. The egg still rests on the bottom, but the water looks slightly cloudier.
tutor_prompts: We added salt but the egg hasn't moved. What does that tell us so far?
What do you think will happen as we keep adding more salt?

steps.instruction: Keep adding salt a tablespoon at a time and stirring, until the egg lifts off the bottom and floats.

checkpoint.expected_state: The egg is floating somewhere above the bottom of the glass — either suspended in the middle or rising to the surface.
success_criteria: The egg is visible and is no longer resting on the bottom of the glass.
There is clear space between the egg and the bottom of the glass.
The water is still in the glass (not spilled out).
failure_hints: Keep going — add another tablespoon of salt and stir well until it dissolves.
Make sure the salt is actually dissolving, not just sitting on the bottom.

simulation.observation: After several spoonfuls of salt, the egg begins to drift upward and finally hovers near the top of the water.
tutor_prompts: The egg floats now even though we didn't change the egg at all. What changed?
Imagine an ocean versus a lake — where would it be easier to float, and why?

reflection: Explain in your own words why the egg sank in fresh water but floated in salt water.
If you used twice as much water but the same amount of salt, would the egg still float? Why or why not?
Name a real-world place where this same effect matters.
